sections.title: What verb patterns are (core logic)
sections.content:
When one verb follows another, English picks **one of three main patterns** — and each verb belongs to a family.
- No strong rules: there are **families of verbs** that take each pattern, and a few verbs whose **meaning changes** with the choice.
- Wrong pattern = unnatural English, even when grammar is otherwise correct.
- This is the Verb Complementation root (#5 in the Tree Model) — learn each verb **with its pattern**, the way you learn a noun with its gender. Start with the families; then nail the meaning-change pairs (the B2 trap).

items.label: to-infinitive
items.formula: verb + to + verb → I want **to leave**.

items.label: bare infinitive
items.formula: verb (no *to*) → I can **swim**. / She made me **wait**.

diagrams.text: Czech mostly uses one infinitive form, so the split feels arbitrary — this is the #1 collocation challenge for Czech and other learners.

sections.title: Verbs + to-infinitive
sections.content:
The **to-infinitive family** — verbs that look forward to an action: a wish, plan, decision, or refusal.

These are the workhorses of intention and planning — every meeting, every conversation about the future uses them.

items.label: after adjectives
items.formula: happy to help / easy to use / difficult to explain

items.label: after too / enough
items.formula: too tired to work / old enough to drive

sections.title: Verbs + -ing (gerund)
sections.content:
The **-ing family** — verbs about an ongoing experience, an activity treated as a thing, or something already in progress.

Czech speakers' single most common error here: *to* + infinitive after these verbs (~~She enjoys to read~~). Fixing this gives an immediate jump in naturalness.

items.label: after any preposition
items.formula: interested **in** learning / good **at** swimming / tired **of** waiting / look forward **to** seeing — here *to* is a preposition, not the infinitive marker

items.label: fixed expressions
items.formula: it's no use / it's worth / can't help / can't stand + -ing

sections.title: Verbs that take EITHER — no meaning change
sections.content:
A small group takes both patterns with **no real difference in meaning** — only a slight stylistic feel. Don't agonise over a fake choice.

Rule of thumb: in British English, **-ing** sounds slightly more natural for general / habitual statements; **to-infinitive** leans formal or specific (one occasion). Don't overthink it.

sections.title: Verbs that take EITHER — with meaning change (B2 trap)
sections.content:
**The big four** — same verb, two patterns, **different meanings**. In every B2 exam (FCE, CAE) as open cloze or sentence transformation.

Get the pattern wrong and you say the opposite of what you mean: *I stopped smoking* (= I quit) vs *I stopped to smoke* (= I paused in order to have a cigarette) — two completely different lives.

diagrams.title: Meaning-change verbs: to-infinitive vs -ing
rows: don't forget to do (future) — *Remember to post the letter.*
fail to do — *I forgot to call.*
have no memory of — *I'll never forget seeing it.*
pause in order to do — *He stopped to smoke.*
cease the activity — *He stopped smoking last year.*
experiment as a strategy — *Try opening the window.*

items.why: = I recall doing it — looking back

items.label: regret + to
items.formula: formal, sorry to say → I **regret to inform** you...

items.label: regret + -ing
items.formula: sorry about the past action → I **regret telling** her.

sections.title: Bare infinitive — special cases
sections.content:
**Bare infinitive** = infinitive **without *to***, used in three clear situations.

Also bare: after *had better* (*you'd better go*) and *would rather* (*I'd rather stay*). **Help** takes either: *Help me **carry*** / *Help me **to carry***.

items.label: 1. after modal verbs
items.formula: can, could, will, would, shall, should, may, might, must → I **can swim**. / She **must leave** now. / We **should go**.

items.label: 2. after let / make / have + object (causative)
items.formula: She **made me wait** an hour. / **Let him go**. / They **had us sign** a form.

items.label: 3. after see / hear / feel / watch + object
items.formula: complete action → I **saw her arrive**. / We **heard him shout**.

items.why: -ing when the action is **ongoing** (in progress, mid-action); bare infinitive for the **complete** action, start to finish

diagrams.text: Czech learners often over-use *to* here. Removing *to* in these cases is one of the highest-leverage fixes for sounding natural.

sections.title: Common Czech-speaker errors + roadmap
sections.content:
High-frequency mistakes (L1 interference + over-generalisation) — they appear constantly in speaking, writing, and FCE/CAE open cloze + sentence transformation. Each fix is small but immediately visible.

**Direct practice here**: gap fill (verb patterns in context) → correct the sentence → MC (key patterns + bare infinitive cases).

Full verb lists by pattern: **Reference → Infinitive and -ing form**. Related: [[topic:phrasal_verbs|Phrasal Verbs]] (verb + particle — another verb-complementation pattern).

items.bad: I look forward to see you.

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Want is followed by to-infinitive, not -ing.

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Enjoy is followed by -ing, not to-infinitive.

questions.question: 'I remember _____ the door.' (I recall doing it.) Which fits?
questions.explanation: Remember doing = recall a past action. Remember to do = not forget to do something.

questions.question: 'I look forward to _____ you.' Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Look forward to is followed by -ing (to is a preposition here).

questions.question: 'She stopped _____.' (She gave up smoking.) Which fits?
questions.explanation: Stop doing = give up. Stop to do = pause in order to do.

questions.question: 'She made him _____ the form.' Which is correct?
'I saw her _____ the room.' Which fits?
questions.explanation: Verbs of perception (see, hear, watch) can take bare infinitive or -ing; bare infinitive often for the complete action.

questions.question: Why is 'I hope see you soon' wrong?

options.b: After hope we need to-infinitive, not the base form alone.
options.c: Hope is followed by that + clause only.

questions.explanation: When one verb follows another, we usually need to + infinitive or -ing; we cannot use the base form alone after hope.

questions.question: 'You'd better _____ now.' Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Fixed phrase 'd better is followed by bare infinitive (no 'to').

questions.question: I want ____ (learn) Spanish.
She enjoys ____ (read) in the evening.
He decided ____ (apply) for the job.
Do you mind ____ (wait) a few minutes?
We hope ____ (see) you soon.
She finished ____ (write) the report.
I'm looking forward to ____ (meet) you.
questions.explanation: Look forward to + -ing (to is a preposition).

questions.question: He promised ____ (help) us. Use the verb in brackets in the correct form (infinitive with or without to).
They avoided ____ (answer) the question.
Don't forget ____ (lock) the door.
questions.explanation: Forget to do = fail to do (future).

questions.question: I'll never forget ____ (visit) Paris for the first time.
questions.explanation: Forget doing = no memory of (past experience).

questions.question: She suggested ____ (go) to the cinema.
She can ____ (swim) very well.
questions.explanation: Modal verbs (can, must, will…) take bare infinitive (no 'to').

questions.question: They made us ____ (wait) for an hour.
I heard him ____ (leave) early.
questions.explanation: Verbs of perception (hear, see, watch) can take bare infinitive.

questions.question: Wrong: 'I want learning English.' Correct the sentence.
Wrong: 'She enjoys to read.' Correct the sentence.
Wrong: 'I look forward to see you.' Correct the sentence.
answers: I look forward to seeing you.
I look forward to seeing you
questions.explanation: Look forward to + -ing (to is a preposition).

questions.question: Wrong: 'He suggested to go early.' Correct the sentence.
Wrong: 'We decided staying longer.' Correct the sentence.
Wrong: 'She finished to write the email.' Correct the sentence.
Wrong: 'She made him to laugh.' Correct the sentence.
Wrong: 'Let me to help you.' Correct the sentence.
'Let me _____ you with that.' Which is correct?
'I'd rather _____ at home tonight.' Which fits?
questions.explanation: Fixed phrase 'd rather is followed by bare infinitive (no 'to').

questions.question: 'We watched them _____ the match.' Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Verbs of perception (watch, see, hear) take bare infinitive or -ing; bare infinitive for the complete action.

questions.question: 'He agreed _____ early.' Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Agree is followed by to-infinitive, not bare infinitive.
